title: Rejected
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She says sheן¿½s very sorry, as she sees you to the gate;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You calmly say ן¿½Good-byeן¿½ to her while standing off a yard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then you lift your hat and leave her, walking mighty stiff and straight, 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But youן¿½re hit, old man, hit hard.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In your brain the words are burning of the answer that she gave,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As you turn the nearest corner and you stagger just a bit;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But you pull yourself together, for a manן¿½s strong heart is brave
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When itן¿½s hit, old man, hard hit.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You might try to drown the sorrow, but the drink has no effect;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You cannot stand the barmaid with her coarse and vulgar wit;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so you seek the street again, and start for home direct,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When youן¿½re hit, old man, hard hit.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You see the face of her you lost, the pity in her smile, 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ah! she is to the barmaid as is snow to chimney grit;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Youן¿½re a better man and nobler in your sorrow, for a while,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When youן¿½re hit, old man, hard hit.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, arriving at your lodgings, with a face of deepest gloom,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You shun the other boarders and your manly brow you knit;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You take a light and go upstairs directly to your room, 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the whole house knows youן¿½re hit.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You clutch the scarf and collar, and you tear them from your throat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You rip your waistcoat open like a fellow in a fit;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And you fling them in a corner with the made-to-order coat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When youן¿½re hit, old man, hard hit.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You throw yourself, despairing, on your narrow little bed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or pace the room till someone starts with ן¿½Skit! cat!, skit!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then lie blindly staring at the plaster overhead, 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You are hit, old man, hard hit.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½s doubtful whether vanity or love has suffered worst,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So neatly in our nature are those feelings interknit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your heart keeps swelling up so bad, you wish that it would burst,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When youן¿½re hit, old man, hard hit.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You think and think, and think, and think, till you go mad almost;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Across your sight the spectres of the bygone seem to flit;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The very girl herself seems dead, and comes back as a ghost,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When youן¿½re hit, like this, hard hit.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You know that itן¿½s all over, youן¿½re an older man by years,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the future not a twinkle, in your black sky not a split.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ah! youן¿½ll think it well that women have the privilege of tears,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When youן¿½re hit, old man, hard hit.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You long and hope for nothing but the rest that sleep can bring,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And you find that in the morning things have brightened up a bit;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But youן¿½re dull for many evenings, with a cracked heart in a sling,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When youן¿½re hit, old man, hard hit.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
